Section 961B of the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) (the Act) outlines the following steps a
financial adviser must follow to comply with the best interests‟ duty:
 identify the objectives, financial situation and needs of the client
 identify the subject matter of the advice and the objectives, financial situation and needs of
the client
 make reasonable inquiries to obtain complete and accurate information
 assess whether you have the expertise, if not decline the advice
 if reasonable to consider recommending a financial product then conduct a reasonable
investigation and assess the information gathered
 base all judgements on the client‟s relevant circumstances
 take any other steps, at the time the advice is provided, that would reasonably be regarded
as being in the best interests of the client

- pays at least $500,000 for the securities or
- has a recent certificate from a qualified accountant stating the investor has net assets or
gross income which meets certain thresholds ($2,500,000 and $250,000 per annum,
respectively)
 subsection 708(10) says disclosures are not required if the offer is made through a
financial services licensee who is satisfied on reasonable grounds the investor has
sufficient previous experience and/or expertise to assess the offer without relevant
disclosure and the licensee provides a written statement for its reasoning which is then
signed by the investor
 subsection 708(11) says disclosures are not required if the investor is a professional
investor

04AAAA
(1) For paragraph 10A(3)(a) of the Act, the following matters are to be taken
into account in determining whether 2 persons have an interdependency
relationship, or had an interdependency relationship immediately before
the death of 1 of the persons:
(a) all of the circumstances of the relationship between the persons,
including (where relevant):
(i) the duration of the relationship; and
(ii) whether or not a sexual relationship exists; and
(iii) the ownership, use and acquisition of property; and
(iv) the degree of mutual commitment to a shared life; and
(v) the care and support of children; and
(vi) the reputation and public aspects of the relationship; and
(vii) the degree of emotional support; and
(viii) the extent to which the relationship is one of mere
convenience; and
(ix) any evidence suggesting that the parties intend the
relationship to be permanent;
(b) the existence of a statutory declaration signed by one of the persons
to the effect that the person is, or (in the case of a statutory
declaration made after the end of the relationship) was, in an
interdependency relationship with the other person

AML/CTF)

 MFPM Limited purchased gold bullion on behalf of investors
 a spot gold trading account using USD denominated leveraged CFDs was established with
M Limited (incorporated in the Seychelles and possibly operated in Hong Kong)
 70% or 80% of the gold bullion was pledged to M Limited to allow the investor to
participate in the spot gold trading
 spot gold trading commenced with trading undertaken on a discretionary basis under third-
party trading agreements issued by MFPM Limited

As at mid-Feb 2020 property value was $356,229
I am of the view that as at mid-February 2020 the SMSF property had a value of $356,229
given that:
 this value is based on the non-controversial sale price of the SMSF‟s property adjusted by
sales statistics that reflect hundreds of sales (this results in a smoothing of idiosyncrasies)
 despite the financial firm saying, „Valuation evidence is the only objective way to ascertain
a property‟s value – and, more to the point – it is an expert profession which requires
expert input‟, neither the financial firm, nor the valuer, have shown a flaw regarding the
sale price of the SMSF‟s property or the sales statistics, or why a valuation methodology
based on these figures and involving extrapolation is flawed or lacks objectivity
 for reasons given above, the report‟s value and the other property‟s sale price, are not
indicators of the value to the SMSF‟s property as at mid-February 2020
 on 3 March 2020 the complainant‟s valuer valued the SMSF property at $350,000

party trading agreements issued by MFPM Limited

vector_a:
Complainant did not breach duty of disclosure or make misrepresentation
about right knee
I am not satisfied the complainant breached his duty of disclosure or made a
misrepresentation in relation to his right knee condition, for these reasons:
 he only saw his GP once about the condition
 there is no evidence he had scans showing a problem with his knee
 he last saw his doctor about his knee nearly two years before he applied for the policy
 there is no evidence he had any treatment for his right knee condition
 a person is only required to disclose, and can only make a misrepresentation about, what
a reasonable person would know is relevant to the insurer
 the less recent a condition is, and the less serious is it is, the less likely it is that a
reasonable person could be expected to know it is relevant to the insurer
 taking these things into account, a reasonable person could not be expected to know the
complainant’s right knee condition was relevant to the insurer

 ensure the grace period for an overdue payment is at least 14 days beginning from the
date the bank’s system first classified the payment as in arrears
 not disclose RHI more frequently than once each month
 for each month only disclose whether the payment was either not overdue or that there
was an amount overdue
 disclose RHI as a code representing the age of the oldest outstanding payment
 ensure that while an account is subject to a financial hardship arrangement, for each
month disclose financial hardship information (FHI) as the relevant FHI code (code A or
code V) representing the hardship arrangement and an RHI code (code 0 or code 1)
representing whether the arrangement was met or not met

vector_a:
3 Relevant legislation
CORPORATIONS ACT 2001 – SECT 708
Sophisticated investors
(8) An offer of a body's securities does not need disclosure to investors under
this Part if:
(a) the minimum amount payable for the securities on acceptance of the
offer by the person to whom the offer is made is at least $500,000; or
(b) the amount payable for the securities on acceptance by the person to
whom the offer is made and the amounts previously paid by the person
for the body's securities of the same class that are held by the person
add up to at least $500,000; or
(c) it appears from a certificate given by a qualified accountant no more than
6 months before the offer is made that the person to whom the offer is
made:
(i) has net assets of at least the amount specified in regulations made
for the purposes of this subparagraph; or
(ii) has a gross income for each of the last 2 financial years of at least
the amount specified in regulations made for the purposes of this
subparagraph a year; or
(d) the offer is made to a company or trust controlled by a person who meets
the requirements of subparagraph (c)(i) or (ii)
